topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the faint, almost invisible watermark on the schematic with a fingertip, her breath catching in her throat. The glow of the monitor reflected in her wide eyes, making them seem impossibly large in the dimly lit workshop. She leaned back, the worn leather of her chair groaning in protest, and slowly pushed herself upright. Her hands, usually steady when manipulating delicate circuitry, were trembling slightly as she compared the handwritten notes beside her to the digital blueprints displayed on the screen. It was absurd. The complexity of the integration sequence, the precise naming conventions for the subroutines—it was all identical. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She glanced toward the doorway, half-expecting someone to materialize, someone to point out the obvious, to shatter this uncanny symmetry. "Impossible," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet space.

[story 2]

Mark kept wiping the condensation from his glasses, his gaze darting between the two physical prototypes resting on the workbench. They were mirror images: the same brushed aluminum casing, the same configuration of internal wiring, the same subtle etching near the power coupling. He picked up a calibration tool, turning it over and over in his palm until the metal felt slick. A prickle ran down his spine, a sensation of profound unease that made him want to bolt. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his mouth making the action difficult. He looked at the timestamp on the initial design file, then back at his own reflection staring back from the polished surface of the casing. "This can't be a coincidence," he muttered, his voice tight, almost a strained rasp. He started pacing the small perimeter of the room, his footsteps echoing too loudly on the concrete floor, searching for an explanation that didn't involve something fundamentally wrong with his perception.

[story 3]

When Liam finally lifted the final component, a sudden, sharp intake of air escaped him. He set the device down with exaggerated care, as if it might explode if handled improperly. The resemblance to the preliminary sketches he’d spent weeks refining was undeniable, terrifyingly so. His palms began to sweat, a clammy dampness spreading across his skin. He stared at the junction point where the primary conduit met the housing, scrutinizing every millimeter. A strange hollowness settled in his chest, a feeling of being profoundly misplaced in this moment. He leaned close to the object, his eyes blurring slightly, trying to reconcile the reality of his creation with the ghost of an identical effort he hadn't consciously acknowledged. "What have I done?" he managed, the question barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system.
